title: NOC 84111 – Silviculture and forestry workers | GTR Immigration
description: Silviculture and forestry workers under NOC 84111 are the "green engineers" responsible for the regeneration and health of Canada's forests. As a TEER 4 occupation, these professionals perform the physical labor required to plant trees, thin overcrowded stands, and maintain forest ecosystems. In 2026, as Canada pursues its "2 Billion Trees" climate commitment, silviculture workers are more essential than ever for carbon sequestration and wildfire prevention. For immigration, this role is a popular entry point through the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level4Major Group84 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionSub-Major Group841 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionMinor Group8411 – Chain saw and skidder operatorsNOC Code84111

duties: Reforestation: Plant trees using manual tools (shovels, dibbles) or mechanical equipment to regenerate harvested or burned forest areas.
Stand Tending: Operate brush saws and chain saws to thin young forest stands, remove competing vegetation, and select high-quality "crop trees."
Nursery Support: Work in forest nurseries to sort, package, and store seedlings for transport to planting sites.
Fire Suppression: Assist in forest fire prevention and suppression activities, including clearing brush for firebreaks and operating water pumps.
Site Preparation: Clear debris and prepare the soil (scarification) for planting using specialized hand tools or machinery.
Maintenance: Maintain tools and equipment and report any disease or insect infestations to supervisors.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
On-the-Job Training: Several weeks of formal and practical training are provided by the employer.

Certifications: * First Aid Certification is mandatory for remote field work.A Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System (WHMIS) certificate is required for handling fertilizers or pesticides.Brush Saw/Chain Saw Safety certification is often required for thinning roles.
A Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System (WHMIS) certificate is required for handling fertilizers or pesticides.
Brush Saw/Chain Saw Safety certification is often required for thinning roles.
High physical stamina and the ability to work in remote, rugged terrain under all weather conditions are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Tree planters are often paid per tree (e.g., $0.15–$0.35 per seedling). Experienced planters in 2026 can earn $300–$600 per day during the peak season.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. With the Canadian government's intensified focus on wildfire management and large-scale reforestation to combat climate change, the silviculture sector is expected to see a 15% growth in job openings over the next three years. The seasonal nature of the work ensures a high volume of recurring opportunities for international workers.
labour_market_demand: The demand for silviculture workers is Very Good, particularly in provinces with massive logging footprints like British Columbia, Ontario, and Quebec. In 2026, there is a record-high demand for Tree Planters and Brush Saw Operators to fulfill federal and provincial reforestation targets. Because the work is often seasonal and physically demanding, there is a constant need for international workers to fill labor gaps in remote forest camps.
Environmental Impact: Directly contribute to Canada’s net-zero goals by planting millions of trees and managing forest health.
Adventure and Outdoors: Ideal for those who enjoy working in remote, pristine wilderness and living in forest camp environments.
Performance-Based Pay: Many roles (especially tree planting) offer "piece-rate" pay, allowing fast, high-quality workers to earn significantly more than the median wage.
Bridge to PR: A recognized "Semi-Skilled" occupation that provides a clear pathway to Permanent Residency in regional immigration programs.
pathways: BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Accessible for workers in the tourism/hospitality and long-haul trucking sectors, but also widely used for forestry workers in specific regions.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for workers with job offers from designated forestry employers in the Atlantic region.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure targeting workers already in Canada, with priority for "Essential Service" roles in natural resources.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Targeted at workers moving to forestry-dependent communities in Northern Ontario and Western Canada.
